translationEn: The sword is more truthful in its tidings than books — in its edge lies the boundary between earnest and jest. Bright sword-blades, not dark pages — on their surfaces lies the clearing of doubt and suspicion. True knowledge is in the meteors of lances gleaming between two armies, not in the seven celestial bodies. Where are the divinations? Where are the stars and what they fashioned of ornament and lies? Fabrications and patched-together tales — neither of nab' wood when counted, nor of gharb. Wonders they claimed the days would unfold in the month of Safar al-Asfar or in Rajab. They frightened people with a dark calamity when the western tailed comet appeared. They made the lofty constellations ranked, whether mutable or fixed. They decree matters on their behalf while the stars are heedless of what revolves in their sphere and axis. Had they ever foretold a matter before its occurrence, they would not have failed to see what befell the idols and the cross. The Conquest of Conquests is too exalted for any verse of poetry or prose of sermons to encompass. A conquest for which the gates of heaven open and the earth comes forth in its finest new garments. O day of the Battle of Amorium — wishes departed from you overflowing, honey-sweet in their yield. You kept the fortune of the sons of Islam ascending while the polytheists and the abode of polytheism declined. A mother for them — had they hoped to ransom her, they would have made every mother and father among them her ransom. And a fortress, bare-faced, whose taming had defeated Khosrau and who had turned away from Abu Karib. A virgin — no hand of calamity had deflowered her, and no ambition of misfortune had climbed to her. Since the time of Alexander or before — the forelocks of the nights grew gray while she did not. Until God churned the years for her as one churns a miser's wealth — and she was the cream of the ages. The black catastrophe came upon them unexpectedly, though its name had been 'the relief of calamities.' An evil omen preceded it on the day of Ankara, when she was left desolate in squares and courtyards. When she saw that her sister had been destroyed the day before, ruin was more contagious to her than mange. How many a valiant horseman lay within her walls, his braids drenched in freshly flowing blood. Stained by the practice of sword and spear in his blood — not by the practice of religion and Islam. You left in her, O Commander of the Faithful, a day when fire humbled both rock and timber. You left in her the darkness of night, though it was bright morning — a dawn of flame illuminating her center. Until the mantles of darkness seemed to have abandoned their color, and as if the sun had not set. Light from fire while darkness brooded, and darkness from smoke in a pale bright morning. The sun rising from one and setting in the other — the sun obligatory from this one though it had not set. Time cleared its clouds for her, revealing a day of battle from her that was pure and holy. The sun did not rise that day upon any married man with family, nor did it set upon a bachelor. The dwelling of Mayya, inhabited, with Ghaylan circling it — is not more beautiful in its hills than her ruins. Nor are cheeks flushed with shame more desirable to my eyes than her dusty cheek. An ugliness that enriched our eyes, sating them beyond every beauty that appeared or wondrous sight. And a beautiful outcome whose consequences endure — its joy came from the evil of its reversal. If disbelief knew how many eras of retribution lay in ambush between the brown spears and the bows. The planning of one who holds fast to God, who exacts vengeance for God, who watches for God, who desires God's reward. A feeder of victory whose spear-points have never been blunt, nor have they been veiled from any hidden soul. He never raided a people or marched on a land without an army of terror preceding him. If he had not led a mighty host on the day of battle, he would have been, by himself alone, a thundering army. God cast you against her towers and demolished them — had anyone other than God cast you, he would have missed. After they had fortified it, confident in it — but God is the key to the door of the impregnable fortress. Their commander said: 'No nearby pasture for those who graze, and the watering-place is not close.' Delusions — the swords' gazelles and the spear-tips' plunder stripped them of their vain hopes' success. Truly the two fates — of white blades and brown spears — have annulled the two lives of water and pasture. I answered a cry from Zubatra — for it I poured out the cup of sleep and the saliva of the dark-lipped beauties. The heat of the oppressed frontiers kept you from the coolness of lips and their sweet fresh water. I answered him, proclaiming with the drawn sword — had I answered with anything other than the sword, I would not have answered. Until I left the pillar of polytheism prostrate in the dust, without pausing for the tent-pegs and ropes. When Theophilos saw the war with his own eyes — and war derives its meaning from ruin — he went about trying to deflect its course with wealth, but the sea with its tides and swells defeated him. Far from it! You shook the firm earth beneath him — a raid of one who seeks God's reward, not one who seeks gain. Gold, abundant as it was, did not avail against the pebbles, for he was in need of gold to buy them off. Truly the lions — the lions of the thicket — their ambition on the day of battle is in the spoils taken, not the plunder offered. He turned away, the spear having bridled his speech, with a silence beneath which his entrails seethed. He gave his closest kin as offerings to sheer death and went on, urging the swiftest of his mounts to flee. Compelled to watch from every high ground, scanning it from the lightness of fear, not from the lightness of joy. If he flees its heat with the ostrich's flight, you have heaped abundant fuel upon its blaze. Ninety thousand — like the lions of the jungle — their skins were roasted before the figs and grapes ripened. O Lord, how many a soul, when their last remnant was cut down, became wholesome — though had it been anointed with musk it would not have been sweet. And many an angry one — the white swords returned him from the living of satisfaction to the dead of wrath. And war standing in a narrow, surging strait, where the standing fighters knelt in humiliation. How much was attained beneath its gleam from moonlit beauty, and beneath its thundercloud from pearly teeth. How much, in the cutting of necks' ties by swords, was there a connection to the secluded virgin. How many Indian blades, drawn bare, were won, swaying amid swaying forms on dunes. White women who, when unsheathed from their veils, were more deserving of whiteness than the veils as companions. O Caliph of God — may God reward your striving on behalf of the root-stock of religion, Islam, and noble lineage. You saw the greater ease and did not perceive it to be attainable except across a bridge of toil. If there is any kinship among the reversals of fate, any connected bond or inviolable covenant, then between your days in which you were victorious and the days of Badr is the closest kinship. It left the sons of the pale ones — as their name suggests — yellow-faced, and it brightened the faces of the Arabs.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Zubatra, a frontier town whose sack provoked the campaign

context: The most celebrated qasida of the Abbasid era, composed in 838 CE to celebrate Caliph al-Mu'tasim's conquest of the Byzantine fortress city of Amorium. The opening line is the most quoted verse in Arabic literature. Abu Tammam opens by refuting the astrologers who predicted the campaign would fail, then narrates the battle with vivid imagery of fire and destruction. The poem compares al-Mu'tasim's victory to the Prophet's victory at Badr, the defining battle of early Islam.

titleEn: Through Muhammad, Time Became Praiseworthy

verses.sadr: وسألت عن أمري ، فسل عن أمره
لو كنت شاهد بذله لشهدت لي

translationEn: Through Muhammad, time has become praiseworthy in my eyes, and it has begun to make amends after its ill conduct. Of refined character — if you were to keep his company, you would see your success in all his qualities. One who loved me with his tongue and his heart, and bestowed upon me with his right hand and his left. He forever produces wondrous things from his elegance, and magnificent gifts from his generosity and bounty. You asked about my condition — then ask about his condition on my behalf, for my state is a piece of his state. Had you witnessed his giving, you would have testified on my behalf that I am either an heir or a partner in his wealth.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: praiseworthy (a play on the name Muhammad)

context: A concise panegyric praising a patron named Muhammad, playing on the meaning of his name (praiseworthy). Abu Tammam declares that through his patron, time itself has become worthy of praise. The intimacy of the poem suggests a close personal relationship between poet and patron.

titleAr: لو أن دهرا رد رجع جواب
titleEn: If Only Fate Would Return an Answer

verses.sadr: لو أن دهرا رد رجع جواب
verses.ajuz: أو كف من شأويه طول عتاب

verses.sadr: من كل ريم لم ترم سوءا ولم
أذكت عليه شهاب نار في الحشا
أو ما رأت بردي من نسج الصبى
لاجود في الأقوام يعلم ما خلا
قوم إذا جلبوا الجياد إلى الوغى
يا مالك ابن المالكين ولم تزل
لم ترم ذا رحم ببائقة ولا
للجود باب في الأنام ولم تزل
لك في رسول الله أعظم أسوة
مهلا بني غنم بن تغلب إنكم
لولا بنو جشم بن بكر فيكم
خذها ابنة الفكر المهذب في الدجى

translationEn: If only fate would return an answer, or long reproach could restrain its two courses, I would have reproached it over two ruins — obliterated — belonging to Zaynab and Rabab. Two women like the twin moons, their radiance surrounded by young maidens like dolls, of the same age. Each a gazelle that intended no harm and did not mix the days of her youth with frivolity. One kindled a flame of fire in the heart with reproach — she who one evening was sister to the clan of Shihab. A reproach resembling madness, as if the bewildered woman had recited half a book. Did she not see my two cloaks woven of youth and see God's dye — and it is my dye? No generosity among people is known of old except a generosity that was the sworn companion of the sons of Attab. An overflowing generosity with which they polished their honors — for magnanimity is the polishing-stone of noble lineage. A people who, when they led their steeds to battle, you were certain the market was a market of blows. O Malik, son of kings — you have never ceased to be called upon in the day of bounty and the day of punishment. You have not struck a kinsman with a calamity, nor have you spoken to your people from behind a veil. Generosity has a door among mankind, and your right hand has always been the key to that door. You saw your people — and their wrongdoing was their doing — wounded by a claw and fang of time. They are the ones who turned those lightning flashes into thunderbolts upon themselves, and that pardon into a whip of torment. So pardon Usama's offense and forgive them, and grant what was due to the All-Granting. They aided you on the Day of al-Kilab and split the water-skins with an overwhelming host. At the spring of Ubagh, they feathered your two arrows of war against al-Harith the fighter. And the nights of al-Hashak and the Tharthar — they brought steeds with lean flanks. Their elders went forth, but their youths managed their affairs — management lacking in soundness. Neither the refinement of the delicate city-dweller nurtured them, nor did they approach the shrewdness of the Bedouins. If you examine them, you find in them nobility of soul and a lack of manners. Let the curtain of your pardon fall upon them generously, and grant them a whiff of your bounty. You have in the Messenger of God the greatest example, the most exalted in tradition and scripture. He gave the hearts-to-be-reconciled their satisfaction generously, and repelled the raiding parties of the confederates. The Ja'farites — their caravan departed from their people, though they were the stars of the tribe. Until parting took its full share from them, and distance separated them from their loved ones. They saw that God's land had cast them out — its sheltering sides — so they returned to repentance. They came to a generous-natured one like you, who pardons the mention of past grudges and grievances. The fool is not the lord of his people; rather, the lord of his people is the one who feigns ignorance wisely. The devil of hypocrisy has been humiliated, and the white swords have silenced the roaring of the lions of the forest. Gather their distant ones to you, for no valley surges without its tributary streams. The arrow flies true with its fitting feathers, and you will not see a tent without poles and guy-ropes. Gently, O sons of Ghanm ibn Taghlib — you are the prey of Adnan and its young falcons. Were it not for the sons of Jushm ibn Bakr among you, your tents would have been raised without domed frames. O Malik, you have entrusted me with a favor whose treasures endure through the ages. O you who court my praise through your generosity — you have indeed courted one seldom courted. Take her — a daughter of refined thought in the darkness, when night's garment is black. A virgin who is inherited in life, and who returns in peacetime though she is rich in spoils. And the passing of nights adds to her freshness, and the aging of days adds to her youthful beauty.
context: A grand panegyric to Malik ibn Tawq al-Taghlibi, governor of a frontier district. Abu Tammam urges Malik to pardon his kinsmen who had wronged him, invoking the Prophet Muhammad's precedent of reconciling hearts. The poem contains vivid battle references to the Days of al-Kilab, Ubagh, al-Hashak, and the Tharthar — famous pre-Islamic and early Islamic engagements of the Taghlib tribe.

titleAr: قل لابن طوق رحى سعد إذا خبطت
titleEn: Say to Ibn Tawq: The Millstone of Sa'd

verses.sadr: قل لابن طوق رحى سعد إذا خبطت

translationEn: Say to Ibn Tawq — the millstone of Sa'd when the calamities of fate grind its heights and depths — you have become their Hatim in generosity, their Ahnaf in forbearance, their shrewdest in knowledge, and their Dughful in genealogy. Why do I see the spacious chamber locked against me, when so often I used to open what was locked? It is as if it were the Garden of Paradise displayed before me, and I have no righteous deed that would admit me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Hatim al-Ta'i, the proverbial model of generosity
al-Ahnaf ibn Qays, proverbial for forbearance

context: A witty short poem addressed to Ibn Tawq, likely a member of the Taghlib family. Abu Tammam praises his patron's virtues by comparing him to proverbial Arab exemplars (Hatim for generosity, al-Ahnaf for patience, Dughful for genealogy), then humorously laments that his patron's door seems locked to him, comparing it to Paradise that requires good deeds for entry.

titleEn: A Journey Drew Near, and Fortune Withdraws and Returns

verses.sadr: ولابد من فرو إذا اجتابه امرؤ
verses.ajuz: غدا وهو سام في الصنابر اغلب

verses.sadr: امين القوى لم تحصص الحرب رأسه
verses.ajuz: ولم ينض عمرا ، وهو أشمط أشيب

verses.sadr: إذا عد ذنبا ثقله منكب امرئ
إذا اليوم أمسى وهو غضبان لم يكن
له زئبر يدفي من الذم كلما

verses.ajuz: بها كان اوصى في الثياب المهلب

translationEn: A journey drew near, and fortune withdraws and returns — those who are healed and befriended forget its nightly travel. Our days are cross-eyed and frowning, unless the vigilant resolve of a well-equipped man guards them. One must have a fur garment — when a man dons it, he becomes, in the bitter cold, lofty and victorious. Secure in strength — war has not shaved his head bare, nor has it stripped him of a life that is hoary and gray-haired. He delights you in valor though he is not reckless, and he grows stubborn against the days when tested. The lands keep casting their blows upon each other and shifting their extremes, while he is kept aside. When the chilled body is clothed in it at dawn, it has beneath it a perspiration dripping profusely. If it counts a sin, its shoulder bears its weight — the inner self says: its beneficence is when it transgresses. I came, when I sought its rebuke, armed with a blow — knowing full well it would soon relent. The thin, windswept man sees it and turns away exhausted, then the morning breeze covers him and turns aside. When garments treat him ill, its word to him whenever he meets it is: welcome and greetings. When the day ends and it is angry, he does not long care when it grows wrathful. As if its upper edges and its waist and lower parts were an ember ablaze. Will you then repay it with the like of its inner fleece — with a gratitude that rises ascending and descends? It has a nap that protects from blame whenever one who wraps himself in it puts it on in an assembly. You are the expert physician — what was the counsel that al-Muhallab gave regarding garments?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: al-Muhallab ibn Abi Sufra, famous Arab commander

context: An unusual descriptive poem (wasf) in which Abu Tammam describes a fur garment in elaborate metaphorical terms, personifying it as a warrior and companion. This type of object-description was a hallmark of the badi' style. The reference to al-Muhallab ibn Abi Sufra at the end connects to an anecdote about that commander's advice on clothing.

titleEn: How Beautiful Are the Days of al-Aqiq!

verses.sadr: أصل كبرد العصب نيط إلى ضحى
فنعمت من شمس إذا حجبت بدت

verses.ajuz: أو صال فيها الدهر صولة مغضب
عمر بن طوق ، نجم أهل المغرب

verses.sadr: في معدن الشرف الذي من حليه
قد قلت في غلس الدجى لعصابة
يغدو مؤمله إذا ما حط في

verses.ajuz: لا خير في الصهباء ما لم تقطب

verses.sadr: صلب إذا اعوج الزمان ولم يكن
verses.ajuz: ليلين صلب الخطب من لم يصلب

verses.sadr: هم رهط من أمسى بعيدا رهطه
verses.ajuz: وبنو أبي رجل بغير بني أب

verses.sadr: ومنافس عمر بن طوق ما له
بحر يطم على العفاة وإن تهج
يا عقب طوق أي عقب عشيرة
قيدت من عمر بن طوق همتي
ومتى امتدحت سواك كنت متى يضق

translationEn: How beautiful are the days of al-Aqiq, and how delightful — and the life in their shade, how wondrous! Their summer where the gazelle herds shelter in its shade, and their spring with its showering rain. A shade cool as dyed fabric attached to a fragrant morning, perfumed with the basil of the meadows. Their luminous shadows cast by young maidens, white, with swelling breasts, whose ankles are concealed. And a dark-eyed one from among the dark-eyed gazelles, nurtured — replaced by a dark-eyed one who was not nurtured. By God, our night — what a night it was — stored up for us between al-Liwa and al-Shurbub. She said, when I had clasped her hand in mine: 'This is lawful, but not every lawful thing is pleasant.' What a sun! When veiled, she appeared from her light as if she were not veiled at all. When she glanced, you would think the gazelles had borne her — a spring gazelle, suckled among the herd. A human if her lineage is traced, but a jinniya of both parents if she is not attributed. I said to the ruined city when it came to lie in the grip of time's fang and claw — a voiceless city in which decay had become an orator speaking in the most eloquent tongue — as if ruin had dwelt in its grounds, or time had charged through it in the charge of one enraged. But the sons of Tawq, and Tawq before them, built the heights of glory with praise most dominant. The world will crumble, yet the edifices of glory and their pavilions remain fresh, not crumbling. Raised by the days of spear-thrusting and glazed with a glittering golden coat of munificence. O you who seek their glory to attain it — far from you is the dust of that procession! You are the one concerned with beauties while seeking the furthest extent of their affection with a gray head. Umar ibn Tawq trampled calamities and curbed their excesses — star of the people of the West. Close-knit in the roots of the spear-shaft, when he claims glory on the day of boasting, rich in soil, of noble descent. In the mine of honor from whose ore the noble qualities of Taghlib daughter of Taghlib were smelted. I said in the darkness of night to a band seeking Abu Hafs: 'Here is the halting-place of the riders.' The Jushamite star is before your eyes — seek the brightness of that star. He gives the gifts of the generous, moist with bounty, freely, then apologizes like one who has sinned. Welcoming to visitors — his cheerful face satisfies you, with no need for family or formal welcome. His hopeful one arrives, and when he alights in his vicinity, his exhausted mount with its worn-out saddle is restored. Smooth is desire and hope at his door — the registry of wishes, the extended shade of aspiration. Seriousness is his nature, but within him is a playfulness — for there is no seriousness in one who never plays. Harsh, then follows that with soft temperament — no good in wine if it is never tart. Firm when time goes crooked — and one who is not firm before a firm calamity will not endure. Affection is for kin, yet his kindness flows to the farthest stranger rather than the nearest relation. Likewise the sons of Attab ibn Sa'd have become the reins of our ever-turning age. They are the clan of one whose kin is distant — sons of a father who has no other sons. And the rival of Umar ibn Tawq has nothing from his rancor but pebbles and rubble. Noble traits and bounty are wearisome, but he whose honor is not tired does not rest. Through his pallor in the pursuit of glory, his face shines — the deeds of one who has not grown pale do not illuminate. A sea that overflows upon the seekers, and if the wind of petition blows, its waves surge. Camels give their milk only when milked, and their udders dry if they are not milked. O descendants of Tawq — what descendants of a tribe you are! — and many a successor has left no successors. I have tethered my ambition upon Umar ibn Tawq — the resolute one, the firm of heart and mind. Praise has found a market at his door, and I have clothed him in a necklace of undrilled ruby. The praise most deserving of refinement is what is composed for a radiant, refined man. His qualities were extraordinary and a poet was extraordinary in praising him — so what a fine rarity within a rarity. When you were noble, I spoke of you with truthful words — I neither sinned nor did I err. And whenever I praised another, I was one who, if truth of speech grew narrow for me regarding him, would become a liar.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: al-Aqiq, a famous valley/wadi near Medina

context: A major panegyric to Umar ibn Tawq al-Taghlibi, whom Abu Tammam calls 'the star of the people of the West.' The poem opens with a nostalgic evocation of days spent at the valley of al-Aqiq, includes a ghazal passage and a meditation on ruins, then transitions into praise of the Taghlib tribe and particularly the house of Tawq. The closing verse is a memorable statement of poetic sincerity.

titleEn: I Settled My Saddle in the Approaching Pasture

verses.sadr: بأتم من قمر السماء وإن بدا
كم أدت الأيام من حدث كفت

verses.ajuz: مثل لها في الروع طعنة فيصل
والماء دون ذي رحم بها متوسل

translationEn: I settled my saddle in the approaching pasture and grazed in the wake of the pouring clouds. Who will inform all the tribes of the Arabs that I built the neighbor before the dwelling? I took hold of a grace whose two folds have not been severed and a covenant that has not been dissolved. Abu al-Walid tore through the darkness with a brilliant face that opened for us the locked door of hope. More complete than the moon of the sky — even if it appeared as a full moon — and more beautiful and comely in the eyes. More eloquent than Quss when you call upon him for counsel, and subtler and more judicious in affairs. A prime of noble eminence that shakes him like a blade is shaken — the prime of a life approaching. So be well for the freshness of a future lordship, untouched, and the coolness of a coming youth. How many a calamity of time his days have warded off — the perplexing calamity of the age. Bearing the burden, discovering it without being perplexed by it — carrying the weight without being burdened. And the great matters — you crushed them with the sharp, keen, resourceful heart. And an assembly where the weapon of speech is eloquence — where speech has a flood that does not clear. Words whose dark and honeyed streams pour, audible — between the alloyed and the intoxicated. You dispelled its darkness with the speech of a decisive one — for which there is, in shock, a likeness to a decisive thrust. It gathered for us the scattered fragments of our hopes from you, by one more dutiful than the spirit of life, and more connected. Favor upon favor on its day, and a favor that has completed a year, and a favor not yet a year old. Like the rain-clouds — from past clouds and approaching ones awaited, and encamped ones dripping. I have a sacred right that has poured your liberality upon me — and water is the provision of him who first draws it. If people marvel that I am among you — without kinship, seeking connection by loyalty — then the Umayyads: al-Farazdaq was their kinsman by lineage, but their affection went to al-Akhtal.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I settled, I took up residence
Quss ibn Sa'ida, legendary Arab orator

context: A panegyric to Abu al-Walid, praising his eloquence, generosity, and youthful vigor. Abu Tammam concludes with a clever analogy: just as the Umayyads were kin to al-Farazdaq but loved al-Akhtal (a Christian outsider), his patron loves him despite their lack of blood ties. This reflects Abu Tammam's own situation as a non-Arab poet seeking patronage.

titleEn: I Saw Nothing but the Venom of Toil

verses.ajuz: قد غربت من غير ما غروب

translationEn: I saw nothing but the venom of constant toil — connecting the night-march with the return journey. More exhausted than weariness and fatigue, she was on the morning of the eastern sun-drenched peak. Responding though she was no purebred camel — with necks arching in wonder. Like night, or like dark things, or like Nubians — submissive, led by an utterly black guide. Like a faction that rallied around the chief — obeying the south wind. Undoing the twists of calamities, warding off the violence of a hostile time. Erasing the clinging crises — an erasure like the touching of the Black Stone erases sins. When she appeared nearby from the earth, the thirsty land gazed at her pouring bounty. As the sick person gazes at the physician, and as the lover yearns for the beloved, and as the joy of a literary man meeting a literary man. She encamped, a truthful rain-shower. The thunder rose within her like a preacher, and the wind moaned like the moaning of she-camels. The sun, with its veiled face, had set without truly setting. The earth donned its fresh garment — in a blooming, moist growth. After the whitening of snow and hoar-frost — like an old man who, after age and bowing, exchanged gray hair for youth. How many a foreign flank she put at ease, and how she overcame the defeated soil, and relieved the distressed sprouting plants. She calmed the fleeing wild animals and contented a fertile land. Preserving the covenant of rain in absence — delicious of lip alongside the morning drink. As if she were raining upon the hearts themselves.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: night travel, setting out in darkness

context: A rajaz poem describing a rainstorm and its effects on the parched earth. Abu Tammam personifies the rain-cloud as a traveler on a journey, then describes its arrival and the transformation of the landscape. The poem is a fine example of his nature description (wasf) poetry, ending with the striking image of rain falling upon hearts themselves.

titleEn: Al-Hasan ibn Wahb
translationEn: Al-Hasan ibn Wahb is like the rain in its pouring. In the prime of his wisdom and the prime of his youth. The abundance of his generosity and the abundance of his surroundings. A noble station that elevated him, and a father who raised him high. We elaborate however we wish in praising him, without any flattery. A robe that he bestowed, like jewelry and its adornment. It drew forth a praise like honey in its sweetness. So he departed wearing my praise, and I departed wearing his garments.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: we flatter him, we show favoritism

context: A short panegyric to al-Hasan ibn Wahb, an Abbasid-era secretary and literary figure who was one of Abu Tammam's most important patrons. The poem is in the rare abbreviated rajaz meter (majzu' al-rajaz), giving it a light, epigrammatic quality. The final verse encapsulates the reciprocal relationship between poet and patron: the patron receives immortal praise, and the poet receives material reward.

titleAr: لا نالك العثر من دهر ولا زلل
titleEn: May Stumbling and Slipping Never Befall You

verses.sadr: لا نالك العثر من دهر ولا زلل

verses.ajuz: من بعض أيدي الضنى واستأسد البخل

verses.sadr: لم يبق في صدر راجي حاجة أمل
verses.ajuz: إلا وقد ذاب سقما ذلك الأمل

verses.sadr: وأعين الخلق تعطي فوق ما سئلت
verses.ajuz: عليك والصبر يعطي دون ما يسل

verses.sadr: حبا بك الله من لولاك لانبعثت
أجر أتاك ولم تعمل له وبلا

translationEn: May stumbling from fate and slipping never befall you, and may glory never be bereaved by your loss. Do not fall ill — for when you are ill, by noble deeds, ailments and diseases appear. Generosity shrank when one of the hands of affliction reached toward you, and miserliness grew bold. No hope remained in the breast of any seeker of a need but that hope melted away in sickness. Then, while the world was in danger and virtue was beseeching God on your behalf, and the eyes of all creation gave more than they were asked to give for you, while patience gave less than was demanded — God bestowed His grace upon one without whom the nights would have launched their journeys, with their trotting and their pacing. A sickness for which a cure was destined, and so it was shattered — as the spear bends at times, then straightens. A complexion that paled, then God restored its freshness — as the star dims a moment, then blazes again. A reward came to you without your having labored for it, and a trial — for the reflection of one who is steadfast in his devotion to God's oneness is itself a deed.
context: A poem composed during the illness of a patron, expressing alarm that generosity itself sickens when the generous man is ill. Abu Tammam transforms a conventional 'get-well' poem into a meditation on the cosmic significance of the patron's health — the world itself is in danger without him. The recovery is likened to a star that briefly dims then blazes anew.

titleEn: Be Patient, O My Soul
translationEn: Be patient, O my soul, for patience is the wisest course. Restrain your grief, for grief, if not curbed, will persist. Clothe yourself in despair of people, for despair is a refuge. Perhaps a hope may fail, and what was not hoped for may arrive. And a letter I wrote — no spy's eye could see it — in which pens poured their ink; it did not reveal a secret, nor was it rolled up in a scroll. But tears answered it, making a mixture for the cup. And a sick-eyed one visited me while the night was drawing its darkness toward me, when the foreigner had prevailed in his power. A sun rose upon us though we had not intended a pilgrimage! Delicious to the taste, it nauseates the touch in the cups. It clothed the old man in youth, so he acquired coquetry and flirtation.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: be patient (feminine imperative, addressing the soul)

context: A poem that begins with philosophical self-counsel — urging the soul to patience and even to embrace despair as a form of liberation. It transitions into a scene involving a secret letter and a nighttime wine-drinking episode. The poem is in the light mujaza' al-ramal meter, giving it a lyrical, song-like quality. The wisdom of the opening verses became proverbial.

titleAr: أبدت أسى أن رأتني مخلس القصب
titleEn: She Showed Grief When She Saw My Temples Gray

verses.sadr: أبدت أسى أن رأتني مخلس القصب
verses.ajuz: وآل ما كان من عجب إلى عجب
إلى المشيب ولم تظلم ولم تحب

verses.sadr: يومي من الدهر مثل الدهر مشتهر
فأصغري أن شيبا لاح بي حدثا
verses.ajuz: وأكبري أنني في المهد لم أشب
فالسيف لا يزدرى إن كان ذا شطب

verses.sadr: لا يطرد الهم إلا الهم من رجل
ماض ، إذا الكرب التفت رأيت له
ستصبح العيس بي ، والليلأ عند فتى
verses.ajuz: كثير ذكر الرضا في ساعة الغضب
وإن تحملت عنه كان في الطلب
وإن ثوى وحده في جحفل لجب

verses.sadr: صيغت له شيمة غراء من ذهب
لما رأى أدبا في غير ذي كرم
verses.ajuz: قد ضاع أو كرما في غير ذي أدب

verses.sadr: من غير ما سبب ماض ، كفى سببا
verses.ajuz: للحر أن يعتفي حرا بلا سبب

translationEn: She showed grief when she saw my temples graying, and what had been a wonder turned to further wonder. Twenty-six years call me, and I follow them toward gray hair — they have neither wronged nor swerved. My day is like an era, famed for resolve and prudence, and my hour is like the ages. So be little amazed that gray has appeared in my youth, and be greatly amazed that I did not go gray in the cradle. Let not the flash of white hair upon me trouble your sleep, for that is the smile of wisdom and refinement. She saw the wrinkling of his skin and her emotions stirred, and her fire of passion told her tears: pour forth! Do not reproach him for the furrowing that covers him — the sword is not scorned if it bears grooves. Nothing drives away worry but worry — from a man who harnesses the desert's she-camels, those swift trotters. Resolute — when crises close in, you see him, through their trotting pace, extending himself over calamities. Tomorrow the white she-camels will bear me by night to a young man who often remembers mercy in the hour of anger. I turned away from him, yet his affection did not turn from me; my good opinion returned to him and was not disappointed. Like the rain: if you come to it, its first drops meet you; if you depart from it, it follows in pursuit. O qualities of al-Hasan — may you endure, for you have become the delight of glory and of noble lineage. As if he is always formed from his own noble qualities, even when he dwells alone amid a great host. He was cast with a nature gleaming like gold — but it is the most destructive of all things to gold. When he saw refinement in one without nobility wasted, or nobility in one without refinement, he aspired to the highest rank, and both joined in his deeds as light and vegetation join. I have tested from you — while my days were blameworthy — a friendship I found sweeter than wealth. Without any prior cause — for the noble man, it is cause enough to approach another noble without cause.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: white hairs, the first signs of graying

context: A poem in which Abu Tammam meditates on his premature graying — he was only twenty-six — and defends it as a sign of wisdom rather than age. The second half is a panegyric to al-Hasan (likely al-Hasan ibn Wahb), praising his rare combination of nobility and refinement. The closing couplet contains a celebrated maxim about noble friendship needing no prior cause.

titleAr: أما أبو بشر فقد أضحى الورى
titleEn: As for Abu Bishr, All People Have Become

verses.sadr: أما أبو بشر فقد أضحى الورى

verses.ajuz: أن ليس أولى من سواه بماله
لو كان يهدي لامرىء ما لا يرى

verses.sadr: أو ما رأيت الورد أتحفنا به
لو كان يهدى لامرئ مالا يرى

translationEn: As for Abu Bishr, all people have become dependents of his gifts and bounty. Whenever you visit him, you return certain that no one is more worthy of his wealth than those other than himself. A nobility that surpasses the noble, beneath which lies a refinement that frees the heart from its shackles. Until, were you to peer into his conscience, you would see — if it were possible to gift what is unseen to a person. I have tested in him a friendship devoted as a slave's, whose arrows were all fletched with his arrows. Did you not see the roses he surprised us with — the gift of one whose friend crossed his mind? Roses like the blush of cheeks, colored with bashfulness, and white in the whiteness of his deeds. And the tawny wine was poured from the finest of what is gathered and what is lawful. A north-wind wine that enriches the impoverished — and that is the true enrichment: overflowing in its scarcity. And a warrior who met death, defeated — death stood red before him. He fell as a champion falls when his days have turned and his heroes are severed from him. He came, and the sharp blades had stripped him of both his spirit and his garment. If it were possible to gift a man what cannot be seen, I would gift him the greatness of his parting and its train. I would return his gift to him — even though it surpasses that — and instead I would ask for some of his noble qualities.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: north-wind wine (cooled by the north wind)
a warrior, one who strips his foes

context: A panegyric to Abu Bishr, praising his generosity and the gift of roses he sent to the poet. Abu Tammam weaves together a scene of wine-drinking, the beauty of roses, and a battle vignette, showcasing his characteristic badi' style of combining disparate images. The poem's central paradox — wishing one could give back an unseen quality — is quintessentially Abu Tammam.

titleEn: The Clouds, Between Evening and Morning Draughts

verses.sadr: دهم اذا ضحكت في روضة طفقت

translationEn: The clouds, between evening drink and morning drink, from the saliva of heavy rain-laden clouds dragging themselves along the earth. Dark clouds — when they smile upon a garden, the eyes of its flowers begin to weep from joy.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: evening drinker, one who drinks in the evening
morning drinker, one who drinks at dawn

context: A remarkably concise two-verse fragment capturing a single image: rain clouds watering a garden, whose flowers 'weep from joy.' The paradox of weeping from happiness is a quintessential badi' conceit. This miniature gem demonstrates Abu Tammam's ability to compress profound meaning into the fewest possible lines.

titleEn: What a Pasture for the Eye, What a Valley of Love Poetry!

verses.sadr: ند عنك العزاء فيه وقاد ال
ربما قد أراه ريان مكسو ال

verses.ajuz: لال في لوعتي ولا في نحيبي
كم بذي الأثل دوحة من قضيب

verses.sadr: ما على الوسج الرواتك من عت
verses.ajuz: ب ، إذا ما آتت أبا أيوب

verses.sadr: ليس يعرى من حلة من طراز ال
فإذا مر لابس الحمد قال ال
كل شعب كنتم به آل وهب
لم أزل بارد الجوانح مذ خض
verses.ajuz: خضت دلوي في ماء ذاك القليب

verses.sadr: ثم لم أدع من بعيد لدى الإذ
verses.ajuz: ن ولم أثن عنكم من قريب
في وداد منكم ولا في نصيب
م على شرح ما به للطبيب

translationEn: What a pasture for the eye, what a valley of love poetry — that the days have stripped bare at Malhub! Youth's passion claimed it, and the settling of decay and time's remnants altered it. Your consolation fled from you there, and tears were led from your eyes like a led horse. Your tears accompanied your longing there — a blood-flow accompanied by a weeping. Clinging to sorrow over separation, and pursuing the far goal of love. After him, the false lightnings of diversion flickered, and clouds of idle flirtation rushed forth. Perhaps I once saw it lush, its haunts dressed in every beauty and fragrance. With sick eyes that are not sick, and suspicious glances that arouse no suspicion. In a noble season of spring and an honorable time of autumn. So peace be upon it — I do not share the ruins in my anguish or my wailing. It is all the same: my answering one who did not call, and my calling in the wilderness to one who does not answer. It is all the same: the ease beneath the night-journey and the song born of suffering, and freshness born of pallor. Ask the white she-camels what they carry, and unite their forms with the desert plains. Do not let your small ambition trail — look how many an oak tree grew from a twig at Dhat al-Athl. The trotting she-camels bear no reproach when they arrive at Abu Ayyub. Resolute — his deeds are no pasture for blame, nor his honor a playground for faults. His speech flows freely when the knot of speechlessness is tied in the orator's tongue. Striking the heart of the matter where perplexities gnaw at the mind of the wise. Not preoccupied with every trifle, nor is every wonder in his sight a wonder. His hand is busy with generosity, his ear alert to every cry of the afflicted. He is never without a garment from the fabric of praise, from a merchant who has profited in it. When one wearing the cloak of praise passes by, the people say: 'Who is the owner of that fresh robe?' When the hand of a seeker is stripped, he departs cheerful like a blazing star. No gazelle of the bridal chamber, stripped of her finery, is more beautiful than a generous man who has been stripped. He loves his friend with the anguish of longing — the love of others for their beloved. Safe in his heart and ribs, when deceit has become the armor of hearts. Not like their pure ones who, when present, show affection, but whose branches wither in absence. He conceals himself from them, but his noble qualities emerge like the emergence of gray hair. Every place where you were, O sons of Wahb, is my place and the place of every man of letters. I have not ceased to be cool at heart since I dipped my bucket in that well. You departed with hardship leaving me, and I became the chosen partner in what is loved. Then I was not called from afar, nor was I turned away from you close by. Each day you adorn my courtyard with unique gifts and rare kindness. My heart for you is like the burning liver, and my heart for others is like ordinary hearts. I do not press a claim of sacred right to gain more affection or a greater share from you. No affliction of reproach strikes a friend except from the friend who eagerly cares. Though the sick one is not blamed for explaining his ailment to the doctor. If we thought emphasis were a sign of weakness, we would not have followed the call to prayer with the second call.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pasture for the eye, a feast for sight
a led horse, one led alongside

context: A long panegyric to Abu Ayyub and the sons of Wahb, likely al-Hasan and Muhammad ibn Wahb, prominent Abbasid secretaries and literary patrons. The poem combines a nostalgic nasib opening at Malhub, a journey section, and extensive praise focusing on the patron's eloquence, generosity, and loyalty. The closing verses express Abu Tammam's intimate bond with the family.

titleEn: How Strange That Your Face Is Turned Away

verses.ajuz: من سوء ما تجنى الظنون ومعقل ؟

verses.sadr: إن تعط وجها كاسفا من تحته

translationEn: How strange, by my life, that your face is turned away from me while you are approaching with the face of your benefit. A kindness you initiated, and a house whose door is open to all creation, yet your face is closed. Do you not see that cheerfulness is a shield against the evil that suspicious minds harvest, and a fortress? The ornament of a favor is that its giver has a word that beautifies it and a lively glance. And an affection folded then spread out — within which there lingers an excuse for its fulfillment. If you give while wearing a gloomy face beneath which lie nobility and forbearance — a nature that does not act in ignorance — then many a rain cloud that passes over you, pouring — its thunder has been generous though it does not smile.
context: A short wisdom poem (hikma) addressing a patron who gives generously but with a cold expression. Abu Tammam argues that a cheerful face is the ornament of generosity — but then concedes that even a thundercloud can be generous without smiling. This diplomatic poem demonstrates Abu Tammam's skill at giving gentle admonishment within the framework of praise.

titleEn: The Gazes of al-Hasan ibn Wahb Are the Sweetest

verses.sadr: وله إذا خلق التخلق أو نبا
verses.ajuz: خلق كروض الحزن أو هو أخصب

verses.sadr: متعت كما متع الضحى في حادث
فإذا طلبت لديهم ما لم أنل
verses.ajuz: أدركت من جدواه ما لا أطلب
في ذاك من صبغ الحياء لمشرب

verses.sadr: وانفح لنا من طيب خيمك نفحة

translationEn: The languorous gazes of al-Hasan ibn Wahb are the sweetest and most bitter in the throat of the envious and most pleasing. His character, when affectation ages or fails, is like the meadow of the firm ground — or more fertile. His virtues have struck the horizon of praise — like musk that is refined by moisture and becomes fragrant. The delicate soul draws out its aroma — fragrant, and it is consumed by the mind and drunk. Generosity led him by its path, and opinions intertwined about him: is it a doctrine or a gilded way? I saw his bright face on the morning of a great calamity and said: is it a lightning flash or a star? I was refreshed as the morning is refreshed in a dark disaster — as if the dawn within it were a sunset. A people sacrifice their reputations for him, who are attended by the worst of faults while generosity is absent. Each one — the shame has been poured from his face as if algae had covered the two pools of his cheeks. With grease-stained garments, who watches his food with a stare sharpened by a hard cheek. When I sought from them what I did not obtain, I attained from his bounty what I did not seek. Youth was joined to youthful zeal by his mantle, and the timely rain of youth watered him. He was purified as a shooting star is purified, yet in that he is tinged with the dye of modesty. Good fortune meets him with his face and loves him, while upon you is the touch of hatred, so feign love. Brotherhood is a birth, and I am a man who befriends wherever I incline, and I produce the best. When men compete in an assembly, some refresh their counsel and others exhaust it. You won both stakes for yourself, and the opinions of the people retreat behind your opinion. When I see you — and speech is pearls that you present — then virgin and previously-set in the necklace. As if Quss were preaching at Ukaz, and as if Layla al-Akhyaliyya were mourning, and Kuthayyir Azza on a day of parting were composing love poetry, and Ibn al-Muqaffa' were elaborating in al-Yatima. You clothe solemnity and startle the dignified at times, and you make listeners weep and rejoice. The gazelle that you gifted us has arrived — artless, and had we wished we would have said: the mount. Soft of finger, with a tongue that is mute — its meanings are silent, but its face is eloquent. It gazes and breaches the hearts with its glance, and it confronts the resistant gaze and becomes companion. The onlookers have pressed the wine of its cheek, and I think it will be made tart by its saliva. Praise you were gifted, and a reward above which soars a phoenix of a dark night. Take it — and even if his kindness is never withdrawn — a pure one, when men are diluted, refined. And bestow upon us from the fragrance of your noble nature a breath, if noble qualities are things that can be given.
context: A rich panegyric to al-Hasan ibn Wahb, one of Abu Tammam's most devoted patrons. The poem praises his noble character, eloquence, and generosity, comparing him to legendary Arab literary figures: Quss the orator, Layla the poet, Kuthayyir the love poet, and Ibn al-Muqaffa' the prose master. The later verses describe a gift of a graceful young attendant (ghulam), reflecting the aesthetics of Abbasid court poetry.

titleEn: The Emir Has Tested You in His Affairs

verses.sadr: فمتى النهوض بحق شكرك إن جنت

translationEn: The emir has tested you in his affairs and found you his truest arrow on the morning of his contest. You supported him in noble deeds and have ever been a pillar for one who holds fast to his rope. So when will you rise to fulfill the duty of thanking you, when your hand has secretly borne for me the fruits of his deeds? I received before you the sweetness of his gift, and I received at your hands the bitterness of his request. When a man extends a favor to you through his influence, it is as if it were from his own wealth.
context: A short poem of thanks to an intermediary who used his influence with an emir to secure a favor for Abu Tammam. The poet acknowledges that receiving through an intermediary involves both sweetness (the gift) and bitterness (the asking). The closing wisdom — that a favor done through one's influence is equivalent to one from one's own wealth — became proverbial.

titleAr: لا خير في قربى بغير مودة
titleEn: No Good in Kinship Without Affection

verses.sadr: لا خير في قربى بغير مودة

translationEn: There is no good in kinship without affection, and how many a stranger's friendship proves beneficial. When kinship approaches with affection, then grasp it with the hand of acceptance, with a helping arm.
context: One of Abu Tammam's most famous wisdom fragments. This two-verse aphorism on kinship and affection became proverbial in Arabic literature, frequently quoted as a standalone maxim. It encapsulates a core theme of Abu Tammam's poetry: genuine affection matters more than blood ties.

titleEn: O Our Days! You Were Nothing But Gifts

verses.ajuz: فما كنت في الأيام إلا غرائبا
إلى ذي الهوى ، نجل العيون ربائبا

verses.sadr: سلبنا غطاء الحسن عن حر أوجه
وجوه لو أن الأرض فيها كواكب
سلي هل عمرت القفر ، وهو سباسب
وغربت حتى لم أجد ذكر مشرق

verses.ajuz: ولو كان أيضا شاهدا كان غائبا
لها الحزن من أرض الفلاة ركائبا
كدرت به نجما على الدهر ثاقبا

verses.sadr: لو اقتسمت أخلاقه الغر لم تجد
verses.ajuz: معيبا ولا خلقا من الناس عائبا

verses.sadr: إذا شئت أن تحصي فواضل كفه
verses.ajuz: فكن كاتبا أو فاتخذ لك كاتبا

verses.sadr: هو الغيث لو أفرطت في الوصف عامدا

verses.ajuz: عليه زكاة الجود ما ليس واجبا

verses.sadr: تحسن في عينيه إن كنت زائرا
خدين العلى أبقى له البذل والتقى

verses.ajuz: إذا ما ذوو الرأي استشاروا التجاربا

verses.sadr: وهل كنت إلا مذنبا يوم أنتحي

translationEn: O our days! You were nothing but gifts, and with the beloved's kindness, you were precious. We shall weep with fresh tears to renew your memory — for in our days you were nothing but extraordinary. And a battlefield of longing where love guided the love-stricken one to the bright-eyed, gazelle-like maidens. Young maidens who visited on short nights, who made me imagine, from their beauty, more maidens still. We stripped the veil of beauty from noble faces that steal the minds of those who stripped them. Faces — if the earth had stars in it that burned for the night-traveler, they would be those stars. Ask — did I not populate the wasteland, though it was empty desert, and leave my encampment a wilderness from my riding-beasts? I went west until I forgot mention of the east, and I went east until I forgot the west. Calamities — when I met them, they left me wounded, as if I had met battle-regiments. He who does not submit to misfortunes finds his own character traits become entirely his misfortunes. The sword that is called 'death' may be blunt, and the man who triumphs may return defeated. The fault of the one is not finding a place to strike; the fault of the other is not finding a striker. And one full of rancor — my climbing to the highest ambition, its hump and its shoulder, seared him. I witnessed the great affairs of glory while he was absent — and had he been present too, he would have been absent. We drove our mounts toward al-Hasan, mounts that made the rugged desert terrain their steeds. I cast my ambition toward him as though I had dimmed, by it, a star shining over fate. I was a man who met time peacefully — then I swore I would meet it only as a warrior. If his noble qualities were divided, you would not find a faulty one nor anyone from mankind who was a critic. If you wish to count the bounties of his hand, be a scribe — or else find yourself a scribe. His gifts are the rain-stars — except that a sign distinguishes those rain-stars from these gifts. He is the rain — if I went to excess in description deliberately, I would never be a liar in praising him. His wealth remained plunder for the heights, and the alms-tax of generosity imposed on him what was not obligatory. You grow more beautiful in his eyes if you visit, and you grow more beautiful still each time you come asking. An intimate of the heights — spending and piety have bequeathed him consequences of kindness that suffice as consequences. His judgment outstrips the slow consultations of experience, when the people of opinion consult experience. I have divorced my hopes — though they were many — at your door. Even if they came to you hunchbacked and exhausted. And was I anything but a sinner, the day I aimed my hopes at someone other than you? Then I came to you repentant!
context: A panegyric to al-Hasan (likely al-Hasan ibn Wahb or al-Hasan ibn Rajab), built on the metaphor of life as a series of gifts from time. Abu Tammam describes his wandering life as a poet seeking patronage — going east and west — before finding his true destination. The closing couplet memorably frames turning to any other patron as a 'sin' from which the poet 'repents' by returning to al-Hasan.

titleEn: O My Shield, My Support, and My Sustainer!

verses.sadr: بل لأمتي ألقى بها حد الوغى
verses.ajuz: بل يا كوكبي أسري به وهلالي

verses.sadr: من شدة الشوق التي قد أفرطت

translationEn: O my shield, my support, and my sustainer! Nay, my southern breeze, fresh, and my northern wind! Nay, my helmet with which I face the edge of battle! Nay, my star by which I travel and my crescent moon! Your absence has shackled the hopes of your brother — an absence that has seized the throat of aspirations. I found it in my ambition and I saw it in my quest and recognized it in my wealth. I came to be overlooked by eyes, diminished, after having been distinguished and notable in your presence. From the intensity of longing that went to excess — so that it appears in the eye as the intensity of my condition. So wash the mote from my eye with lines of writing that lift the cares from my weary heart. Black lines that whiten the face with the choicest of those anecdotes and proverbs of yours. And urge your generous fingers among them, so that they may roam there in every field of play. They have never ceased to be the nursing-mothers of all eloquence, and the cradlers of beauty and grace. Within the belly of a cheap sheet of paper whose insides contain the pearls of precious speech. I count you a fortress the like of which no refuge matches, nor any mountain among the mountains. And I find your letter, bearing greetings, sufficient in place of others' letters bearing gifts and wealth.
context: A poem of longing addressed to a close friend or patron whose absence has left Abu Tammam feeling diminished. He begs for a letter as consolation, describing the friend's fingers as 'nursing-mothers of eloquence.' The poem reveals the deep importance of literary correspondence in Abbasid intellectual culture, where a letter from a friend could substitute for material gifts.

titleEn: Praise Be! — From a Rain-Star Worthy of Praise

verses.sadr: كم حملت لم قتر من زاد

translationEn: Praise be! — from a rain-star worthy of praise, in the scorching midday heat, not the cold stretches. Released from freezing cold and driving storms, it came urged onward — what a fine driver! A night-traveling cloud, generous in yielding, sleepless in the valley while it rains. It gave the earth victory over what it besieges — a descent at the satisfaction of the servants. It set an ambush for the drought. It was driven by a lightning of blazing spark, as if it were the glinting of sheathed swords. Then by a thunder of clamorous roaring that lashes the clouds with sharp tongues. When it marched to answer the land's need, the rear caught up with the vanguard and darkness mixed with darkness. It gave the earth victory over its enemy, and watered its thirsty skulls. How much provision it carried without stinting, and how much nourishment for a barren year. And it milked from the prime of its store — from the sturdy she-camels and the resilient ones. And the choice, purebred mares — and from the fine striped Yemeni cloaks. Of deep red and other hues — a gift from a Generous One, self-sufficient. Neither born nor bearing, withheld from the settled and the nomad alike. Until it settles in the moist lowland.
context: A rajaz poem describing the arrival of life-giving rain after drought. Abu Tammam personifies the rain-cloud as a generous warrior who defeats the enemy of drought. The attribution of rain to God's generosity — 'neither born nor bearing' — echoes Quranic language. This piece exemplifies Abu Tammam's mastery of nature description in the rajaz meter.

titleEn: I Follow My Impulses — I Am Not Ruled by My Reproacher

verses.ajuz: فإن أنت لم ترضي بذلك فاغضبي

verses.sadr: ولي بدن يأوي ، إذا الحب ضافه
تصدع شمل القلب من كل وجهة
لو ان امرأ القيس بن حجر بدت له
verses.ajuz: لما قال مرا بي على أم جندب

verses.sadr: كأن له دينا على كل مشرق
verses.ajuz: من الأرض أو ثأرا لدى كل مغرب

verses.sadr: له كرم لو كان في الماء لم يغض
verses.ajuz: وفي البرق ماشام امرؤ برق خلب

verses.sadr: إذا قال أهلا مرحبا نبعت لهم
verses.ajuz: مياه الندى من تحت أهل ومرحب

verses.sadr: ذوون ، قيول لم تزل كل حلبة

verses.ajuz: وجدت المنايا منه في كل مضرب

verses.sadr: تركت حطاما منكب الدهر إذ نوى

verses.ajuz: بها وبنو الآباء فيها بنو أبي

verses.sadr: فقومت لي ما اعوج من قصد همتي
verses.ajuz: وبيضت لي ما اسود من وجه مطلبي

translationEn: I follow my impulses — I am not ruled by my reproacher, and my led camel, if you blame me, does not obey. So do not send your anger to one who clears himself, and do not bring reproach to the courtyard of one who will make amends. I have accepted passion and longing as a boon companion, so if you do not accept that, then be angry. The turning states of separation turn me upon the harsh states of sorrow and tribulation. I have a body that, when love visits it, seeks shelter in a burning liver and a tormented heart. And a maiden — willow-like, sun-like, gazelle-like — slender in her upper form, full in her hips. She shatters the gathering of the heart from every direction and splits it with grief from every splitting-place. With an intoxicating, still, dark eye, and a pure, clear, pearly-toothed mouth. From the women who give beauty and receive it — whether robed or with a trailing garment not yet robed. Had Imru' al-Qays ibn Hujr seen her, he would not have said 'Pass by me to the abode of Umm Jundab.' That is my concern — not your seeking harm against my dwelling. Come not at dawn if you do not come at nightfall. Did you attempt to guide me? My mind is my guide. Or did you seek to discipline me? My fate is my teacher. They two darkened my condition, then cleared their two darknesses from the face of a beardless youth with gray hair. A thorn in the throat of misfortunes — his resolve shines in the east while it sets in idle matters. As if he had a debt owed by every eastern land, or a blood-claim in every western one. I saw in Ayyash qualities that could not be complete except in the refined and cultivated. He possesses a generosity that, were it in water, would never recede — and in lightning, no man would ever see deceptive lightning. A brother of adversities — his giving is the giving of a benefactor toward us, but his apology is the apology of a wrongdoer. When seekers come to him, they find his pools full and find his garden never barren. When he says 'Welcome,' the waters of generosity spring from beneath the words 'Welcome and greetings.' It awes you to find him the head of an assembly, the breast facing enemies, and the heart of a procession. A bastion — around whose pillars the clans of Hadramawt and Ya'rub have rallied for protection. By one most bold in overcoming every bold man, and most victorious in advancing over every victor. As their allegiance in the past to his forefathers — to the one of kindness and renown, Qayl and Marhab. Champions, horses — in every race, one of them tears forth: a radiant, swift-flanked stallion. A hero like the blade of a sword — however you brandish him, you find death in every striking-edge. I left the shoulder of fate in ruins when it intended to crowd me, once I made you my shoulder. It was not the narrowness of the earth's regions that drove me to you — rather, my path in you is my true path. You are in Egypt my destination and my kinship there, and the sons of my fathers there are the sons of your father. It is no wonder that you smoothed the slopes of my pasture for my camels to roam freely, and made my watering-place abundant. You straightened for me what was crooked in the course of my ambition, and whitened for me what was blackened in the face of my aspiration. Here are the garments of praise — drag their trains upon you — and here is the mount of commendation — so ride!
context: A grand panegyric to Ayyash, a patron in Egypt. The poem opens with a defiant nasib rejecting the reproacher's interference in matters of love, references Imru' al-Qays (the father of Arabic poetry), then praises Ayyash's Hadramawti and Yemeni lineage. The poem documents Abu Tammam's time in Egypt, where he declares his patron's land is his true homeland regardless of blood ties.

titleEn: I Am Bishr — I Have Opened a Door

verses.ajuz: به ، أم من أفدت به الجزيلا !

translationEn: I am Bishr — I have opened a door and have nearly completed it, save for a little. It has now become a mighty tree, though I remember when, months ago, it was still called a sapling. I do not know who is higher in deeds, and who builds the heights in breadth and length — is it the one who gives abundantly without counting the favor, or the one through whom I gained what is immense? I saw you wrestle with needs until you render the most stubborn of them docile. You answer every one who calls you to the heights, crying: O Abd al-Hamid! O Bajila! His is the immense gratitude over the enemies, when the gratitude of other men has become puny. If you could see Kindness as a face, you would see it beautiful and comely.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: counting the favor, reminder of gift

context: A panegyric spoken in the voice of Bishr, addressed to Abd al-Hamid of the Bajila tribe. The central image compares a project or patronage relationship to a palm sapling that has grown into a mighty tree in just months. The closing verse — 'if you could see Kindness as a face, you would see it beautiful' — is one of Abu Tammam's most-quoted lines, exemplifying his personification technique.

titleEn: The Hand of Complaint Has Come to You by Post
translationEn: The hand of complaint has come to you by post, extended by poems in song. It turns between its fingers a new hope, wearing the two garments of fresh desire. I complained to Time of my body's wasting, and it guided me to Abd al-Hamid. So I came to you riding the steed of my verses, in full confidence, from the distant land.
context: A short poem sent to Abd al-Hamid as a petition, likely accompanying a longer message by the Abbasid postal system (barid). Abu Tammam personifies his complaint as a hand reaching out through poetry. The poem is a miniature of the petition genre, where poets sent verses ahead of them to patrons in distant cities to prepare the ground for a visit.
